title: Ukraine's elite special operators are doing more infiltration missions as the front-line kill zone grows
description: Infiltration missions consist of small groups of soldiers who sneak past enemy lines to carry out raids or assault operations.
fullText:
Ukraine's elite soldiers have increased the number of infiltration missions they have conducted over the past year.

The uptick in missions comes as the drone-infested kill zone around the front line grows larger.

A Ukrainian special operator attributed this trend to a rise in Russian drones over the battlefield.

Elite Ukrainian special operators are carrying out more infiltration missions into Russian-held territory as the drone-infested kill zone continues to grow, making larger maneuvers difficult.

Infiltration operations typically involve small groups of soldiers sneaking past enemy lines to complete a mission, which can range from taking a key position to conducting ambushes, raids, and assaults.

For Ukraine's Special Operations Forces, "the number of missions has increased over the past year," an operator in the 4th Ranger Regiment told Business Insider in a recent interview.

The expansion of the kill zone has the Ukrainians not only executing more infiltration-style missions but also tasking operators to confront the challenges posed by similar Russian activities.

The 4th Ranger Regiment operator, who is the second-in-command of a small unit and requested to be identified only by his call sign "Gur" for security reasons, said he tends to receive "specific, targeted missions."

"We might be tasked with destroying one particular enemy position. We go in, destroy it, and come back out," Gur said, per a translation of his remarks. "That might take two days, four days, or a week. We don't necessarily have to stay in the area for a fixed amount of time."

To manage the increased tempo, Ukrainian special operators will rotate teams for these missions, allowing soldiers to rest, train, and prepare.

Ukrainian soldiers and officials often describe the front line as a "kill zone," an area heavily saturated with drones that can quickly attack troops and military vehicles, making any movement very dangerous. Drones are responsible for roughly 90% of all battlefield strikes.

The kill zone varies in width across the battlefield, though it typically extends roughly 10 to 20 kilometers in either direction from the line of contact. However, Ukrainian soldiers and officials have said it is expanding, forcing commanders to make tactical adjustments.

Gur attributed the expanding kill zone to the further proliferation of first-person-view (FPV) drones — cheap quadcopters rigged with explosives to deliver precision strikes. Ukrainian infiltration missions have increased concurrently with similar Russian operations.

"They're using our tactics as well," the operator said. "They infiltrate in groups of two to four soldiers, and they can make their way into our rear areas. Then that becomes another task for us. We have to go in and eliminate them."

Ukrainian soldiers said last year that Russian infiltration had become increasingly problematic during the war. The missions varied; some focused on holding positions, while others targeted key defenses. But they were largely designed to distract and cause chaos.

A senior Ukrainian defense official told Business Insider in late 2025 that small Russian infiltration teams were exploiting bad weather to sneak into Ukrainian-held territory and sow trouble. These missions are high-risk and can be deadly for those involved.

Front-line Ukrainian units don't always have the numbers to effectively cover all the territory they hold, Gur said. So when the Russians infiltrate, his group may get assigned to handle the breach.

"Basically," he said, "the Russians are our task."

The expanding kill zone has also pushed Ukrainian special operators to start using ground robots for logistics closer to the front lines and to evacuate casualties, both of which have become too dangerous for soldiers with the current drone presence.

"War creates its own conditions. FPV drones appeared, so we have to react to that," Gur said. "We have to adapt somehow if we want to survive and keep operating."

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: 'Interaction' with Iran's injured Supreme Leader increasing daily, president says
description: DUBAI, July 21 (Reuters) - The level of "interaction" with Iran's Supreme Leader is increasing day by day, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said in a video clip published on state media on Tuesday.
fullText:
DUBAI, July 21 (Reuters) - The level of "interaction" with Iran's Supreme Leader is increasing day by day, Iranian President Masoud ‌Pezeshkian said in a video clip published on state media ‌on Tuesday.

His remarks follow months of speculation about the health of Supreme Leader Mojtaba ​Khamenei, who has not been seen in public since the airstrike that killed his father and predecessor, Ali Khamenei, at the start of the Iran war.

"The interaction level with our dear Supreme Leader is increasing ‌day by day and ⁠should increase so that we can solve our problems with his guidance," Pezeshkian said in the clip.

Mojtaba ⁠Khamenei suffered severe facial and leg injuries in the February 28 attack, three people close to his inner circle told Reuters in April.

Khamenei ​has issued ​written statements since his appointment as ​Iran's third supreme leader by ‌a clerical body on March 8, but no new image or voice recording has accompanied them.

Senior sources told Reuters in July that the lack of an image or a voice recording was due to health and security considerations. The security risks are substantial given ‌the assassination of his father in the ​first U.S. and Israeli strikes, which ​were launched during diplomatic efforts ​to resolve disputes between the countries.

Mojtaba Khamenei owes ‌his elevation to Iran's Revolutionary Guards, ​who backed him ​as a reliable guardian of their hardline agenda. Already strengthened by war, the Guards' growing dominance signals a more aggressive foreign ​policy and tighter ‌domestic repression, sources familiar with the country's inner policy-making circles ​told Reuters in April.

title: Tokyo urges men to wear shorts to work, but some women say it's 'leg hair harassment'
description: The Tokyo Metropolitan Government wants workers to ditch the suit and tie for t-shirts, trainers - and shorts.
fullText:
As temperatures rise this summer, Japanese men are baring more leg at work than ever before.

The Tokyo Metropolitan Government is pushing company employees to ditch their usual suit and tie for more casual attire, featuring t-shirts, trainers - and shorts.

Tokyo Governor Yuriko Koike introduced "Tokyo Cool Biz" in April, hoping to expand on her signature cool down initiative which has now become a widely recognised annual summer tradition.

Almost four months in, the newly allowed shorts in the office policy is getting mixed reviews.

Some commentators enjoy the relaxed rules, helping them work more comfortably. Others say the policy is unfair to women since they are still expected to wear tights when showing a part of their legs. And a few women describe the experience of "leg hair harassment" or a term coined online as "sunehara", referring to the discomfort of being forced to see their colleague's leg hair.

"We want to give people more options in the severe heat, not tell them what to wear. There should be no issue as long as the work attire is not offensive to anyone," Tokyo Metropolitan Government environmental official Noboru Watanabe told the BBC.

A June survey by Gorilla Clinic found 53.5% opposed wearing shorts to work in the summer while 46.5% were in favour of the new recommendation.

For those against shorts in the office the main reason, for both genders, was the concern over body shape and body hair. The clinic says women significantly outnumbered men in their response, indicating that women may be more resistant to seeing a male colleague's bare legs.

In recent years more Japanese workers, notably those working at startups and tech companies, have started wearing casual clothes. But this year, the Tokyo government recommendation has made it more socially acceptable. Still, some men have been feeling self-conscious about their leg hair.

Akifumi Funatsu, the director of Gorilla Clinic, says he has noticed more clients coming to the clinic not just for cosmetic reasons, but as a matter of "social etiquette when wearing shorts". He thinks women believe that "men should have less leg hair", which has prompted them to seek laser hair removal so as not to offend those around them.

Koike first introduced the summer wardrobe shift as Japan's environment minister in 2005. She launched a national energy-saving campaign called "Cool Biz" to encourage company workers to opt for short-sleeve shirts.

Government leaders participated in the initiative, with then Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi often seen in public without wearing a tie or jacket, helping get the word out.

A few years later following the Tohoku earthquake and tsunami in March 2011, the government launched "Super Cool Biz" which expanded the guidelines to help reduce energy use both at work and at home.

As with other parts of the world, Japan has been experiencing record heat in recent months while grappling with soaring energy prices driven by instability in the Middle East.

Forecasters have warned of extreme heat this summer, with the Japan Meteorological Agency trying to raise public awareness by announcing the word "kokusho-bi", which means "brutally hot day" when the mercury exceeds 40C.

The latest weekly data shows seven people died in Japan from heatstroke and 4,580 people were admitted to hospitals in the seven days from 6-12 July, according to the Fire and Disaster Management Agency.

Besides staying hydrated and turning on the air conditioning, some of the most basic actions repeatedly encouraged by government officials, residents have turned to new products and gadgets to beat the heat.

Items such as clothes with built-in fans, disposable wet-towels, UV and heat blocking umbrellas, and portable handheld fans have been popular and sold in shops nationwide.

Experts from the Japan Academic Network for Disaster Reduction are stressing the need for heat acclimatisation as it takes several weeks for the body to adapt to high temperatures.

They say the key is to start preparing for hot weather and taking measures to prevent heatstroke during the country's rainy season, before the full summer heat arrives.

Japan also faces high humidity which makes it harder for sweat to evaporate, reducing the body's ability to cool itself.

title: Nicaragua's Ortega says 'never again' for elections after two decades in power
description: July 20 (Reuters) - Nicaragua's long-serving president Daniel Ortega said the Central American country will no longer hold elections, eliminating the possibility of an electoral challenge when his
fullText:
July 20 (Reuters) - Nicaragua's long-serving president Daniel Ortega said the Central American country will no longer hold elections, eliminating the possibility of an electoral challenge when his term ends next year and further entrenching the rule he shares with ‌his wife.

Ortega, who has continuously held power since 2007 following an earlier presidential term in the 1980s, said the move ‌will close off any path to the opposition, although he did not provide details about carrying out the ban.

"There won't be any more elections here for them to ​try to seize the government and seize power," Ortega said in a speech on Sunday evening to commemorate the 1979 Sandinista revolution, in which Ortega helped overthrow the U.S.-backed Anastasio Somoza dictatorship.

"The days when parties backed by the Yankees (United States) and the Somocistas would return to power are over - never again," he added, in the 80-year-old's first public appearance in two months.

The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for ‌comment. The Trump administration has described the Ortega ⁠government as a dictatorship, and imposed sanctions on more than 2,350 Nicaraguan officials and their relatives.

Nicaragua's last election in 2021 was widely disputed after Ortega detained opponents and business leaders and criminalized dissent.

Last year, Ortega further ⁠consolidated his power through a series of constitutional reforms that included naming his wife Rosario Murillo as "co-president" and extending the presidential term to six years. He and his wife control virtually every aspect of the government, including the armed forces and the judiciary.

The U.N. Human Rights Council has accused Ortega ​and ​Murillo's government of transforming the country "into an authoritarian state" and systematically violating human ​rights. In 2018, Ortega responded to widespread anti-government protests ‌with a crackdown in which more than 300 people were killed.

Analysts and opposition politicians in exile said the declaration put into focus the reality of two-person rule under the Ortegas, while revealing the couple's fear of an opposition movement taking root.

"Ortega and Murillo are evidently afraid at the idea that the slightest political opening could create the conditions for dissent to manifest and threaten their grip on power," said Tiziano Breda, a senior analyst at Armed Conflict Location & Event Data, a nonprofit that tracks political violence. "Instead of staging a rigged election, they have ‌opted to eliminate electoral competition altogether."

The country has transformed into a "full-fledged, family-run dictatorship," ​he added.

Felix Maradiaga, a presidential hopeful for the opposition who was jailed in ​Nicaragua from 2021 to 2023 before being expelled to the ​U.S., said he saw Ortega's comments less as a show of force than as "a confession of fear."

"Ortega publicly ‌acknowledged what has always been his true intention: to prevent ​his tyrannical project from being subjected ​to any form of democratic competition," Maradiaga told Reuters.

Ortega had already effectively squashed competition during prior reelections of 2011, 2016 and 2021, according to Salvador Marenco, coordinator of the Costa Rica-based Nicaragua Nunca Más Human Rights Collective.

And last year's constitutional reforms had ​already established a one-party system in a country ‌where opposition leaders no longer exist, because they have been exiled.

Ortega, he said, is trying to "avoid the risk of ​an electoral defeat, because he knows that the people reject him."

title: West Nile Virus found in Pittsburgh. Is mosquito-borne illness common?
description: Mosquitos in the Pittsburgh area tested positive for West Nile Virus. These are the symptoms and how you can prevent it.
fullText:
Mosquito samples in the Pittsburgh area recently tested positive for West Nile Virus, a mosquito-borne disease that causes about 2,000 diagnosed illnesses in people each year — a number the Centers for Disease Control says is an undercount.

The Allegheny County Health Department will spray for mosquitos on July 22 in several areas around the county, according to a press release from the department. They'll spray when mosquitos carrying West Nile Virus are most likely to be active.

Allegheny County will treat Edgewood, Garfield, Homewood, Point Breeze, Regent Square and Wilkinsburg for mosquitos.

Mosquitos will be treated with Zenivex E20, one of the safest insecticides approved for use by the state department of environmental protection, according to Allegheny County Health Department.

The pesticide is sprayed in a way meant to reduce the amount used, according to the county health department, and it works only when it comes in contact with mosquitos. It also degrades rapidly in sunlight.

The pesticide will be sprayed from trucks on Wednesday, July 22 from 8 p.m. to 11 p.m. when the species of mosquitos carrying West Nile Virus are most active. If rain occurs, pesticide will be sprayed the following evening during the same hours.

West Nile Virus is most commonly spread to people through the bite of an infected mosquito during mosquito season, though it can also spread through organ transplantation, blood transfusion, exposure in a laboratory setting and from mother to baby during pregnancy, delivery or breastfeeding, the CDC said. Often, it doesn't cause symptoms, or it results in minor illness. Rarely, the illness can develop into a neuroinvasive disease.

Every year, about 2,000 people in the United States are diagnosed with West Nile Virus, though many likely go undiagnosed because they do not have symptoms or attribute mild symptoms to other causes, according to the CDC.

So far in 2026, there has been one case of West Nile Virus in Allegheny County — Pennsylvania's only reported case this year, according to the CDC.

Overall, there have been 81 reported cases of West Nile Virus infections in people in 18 states this summer as of July 21. Of those, 57 cases involved neuroinvasive disease, a more rare symptom type of West Nile Virus that involves neurological symptoms.

Most reported cases were in Arizona and Texas, with Arizona reporting 50 cases and Texas reporting seven. Other states reported one to two cases.

There are precautions people can take to help prevent mosquito bites, including bites from the species carrying West Nile Virus.

"To deter mosquito bites, we encourage people to use insect repellent on exposed skin or to wear long sleeves and pants. Both methods are effective at reducing or eliminating the possibility of getting a mosquito bite," said Allegheny County Health Department Vector Control Specialist Nicholas Baldauf.

He also added that stopping mosquitos from reproducing is an important part in reducing the county's mosquito population.

"Mosquitoes can breed in as little as a half inch of stagnant water, so residents should pay close attention to potential breeding sites like stagnant water in tires, unused swimming pools, buckets, corrugated piping and clogged gutters," Baldauf said.

If people see mosquito breeding sites on private or public properties, they should report those sites to Allegheny County Health Department's Housing and Community Environment Program at 412-350-4046.

West Nile Virus symptoms can appear two to 14 days after exposure, with an average incubation range of two to six days, according to the CDC. There's the potential for a longer incubation period in immunocompromised people. About 80% of infections in people are subclinical or asymptomatic.

For those with symptoms, West Nile Virus can cause two types of illnesses: Non-neuroinvasie disease and neuroinvasive disease. Non-neuroinvasive disease is more common, with symptoms such as:

Transient maculopapular rash (red, bumpy skin)

Neuroinvasive disease impacts less than 1% of people infected with West Nile Virus, with elderly individuals and immunocompromised people most at risk. It generally manifests as meningitis, encephalitis or acute flaccid myelitis.

There is no cure for West Nile Virus, and treatment is supportive, according to the CDC. No vaccine is available for use in people.

Finch Walker is the Pittsburgh Connect Reporter for the USA TODAY Network. Contact Walker at FWalker@usatodayco.com. Instagram: @finchwalker_. X: @_finchwalker.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: West Nile Virus found in Pittsburgh. Is mosquito-borne illness common?

title: UK politician Ann Widdecombe was struck 21 times with a hammer, court hears
description: Ann Widdecombe, the former British lawmaker found dead at her home in southwest England in early July, was hit 21 times with a hammer, a London court heard
fullText:
Ann Widdecombe, the former British lawmaker found dead at her home in southwest England in early July, was hit 21 times with a hammer, a London court heard on Tuesday.

Joshua Kerry, 28, allegedly entered Widdecombe's house in the village of Haytor Vale on July 8, Prosecutor Kashif Malik told Westminster Magistrates' Court, according to UK news agency PA Media.

He then allegedly walked into the 78-year-old politician's kitchen wielding a hammer and repeatedly struck her head while she was eating lunch, the court heard.

He was only in her home for two minutes, the court heard.

Widdecombe's provisional cause of death was described in court as a "blunt force injury to the head."

Kerry then appeared at London's Old Bailey criminal court, where he was denied bail and remanded in custody. A case management hearing is scheduled for October 9, and a provisional trial date is set for 8 June 2027.

British counterterrorism police took over the investigation into Widdecombe's killing on July 13, after officers initially stated there was no information to suggest her death was an act of terrorism.

Her death came against a backdrop of increased violence and heightened threat against lawmakers in Britain. She is the third Member of Parliament to have been killed in recent years, following Sir David Amess and Jo Cox.

title: White House lawyer confirmed to judgeship after bashing Republican senators
description: A White House lawyer nominated by President Donald Trump to serve on a court that hears litigation regarding trade laws and tariff policies was confirmed to the
fullText:
July 21 (Reuters) - A White House lawyer nominated by President Donald Trump to serve on a court that hears litigation regarding trade laws and tariff policies was confirmed to the bench on Tuesday after she apologized for past social ‌media posts that attacked various senators including Republicans.

The Republican-led Senate voted 50-48 in favor of Kara Westercamp becoming a life-tenured judge ‌on the U.S. Court of International Trade, with Republican Senator Susan Collins of Maine joining with Democratic lawmakers to oppose her confirmation.

Two Republican senators did not vote, including Senator Mitch ​McConnell of Kentucky, who has been hospitalized and has not been present for votes. Westercamp has previously in Twitter posts referred to McConnell as "Cocaine Mitch" and accused him of betraying Trump.

The Senate moved ahead with votes on Westercamp while McConnell was out following the July 11 death of another of her social media targets, Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, whom she called "weak-ass" and "disappointing." She had retweeted a post critical of Collins as well.

Westercamp's Twitter posts as ‌well as her role advising Trump on tariff ⁠policies became central topics for senators as they weighed whether she should be allowed to serve on the New York-based specialist court, which hears disputes concerning customs and international trade laws.

Westercamp, an associate counsel in the White House, ⁠had previously worked at the U.S. Department of Justice. Trump in announcing her nomination in a February social media post touted her as a "very experienced Trade Lawyer" involved with "defending our historic and popular America First Agenda."

Trump announced her nomination a week before the U.S. Supreme Court struck down his sweeping global tariffs that had ​previously ​been faulted by the Court of International Trade.

Westercamp has said she was not ​involved in the initial formulation of the Trump tariff policies ‌at issue in the Supreme Court case but has said she was involved in drafting and reviewing some executive orders that had also relied on International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

She has vowed to recuse herself from matters she worked on while at the White House Counsel's Office.

Westercamp also faced questions during a March hearing before the Senate Judiciary Committee about what Senator Dick Durbin, the panel's top Democrat, called "prolific, inflammatory" social media posts attacking various senators.

"This shameless display of extreme rhetoric does not reflect the independence or the judicial temperament required for a lifetime appointment to the federal ‌bench," Durbin said in a statement on Tuesday.

Westercamp apologized during the March hearing and said ​she made those posts in her personal capacity while working at the Justice Department.

"In ​hindsight, I think that using Twitter is not the right platform, ​especially retweets from someone I don't know regarding political opinions, controversial topics, or even characterizations of people," she said. "And ‌so I do sincerely apologize for those posts."

Senate Democrats at ​the hearing also pressed Westercamp on ​other social media posts in which they said she suggested law enforcement or Antifa was complicit in the violence that occurred during the January 6, 2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol, when thousands of Trump supporters mounted an unsuccessful attempt to overturn Trump's 2020 ​electoral defeat.

Westercamp said she condemned all violence that occurred ‌that day and regretted her social media posts. Asked who won the 2020 election, Westercamp would only say that "in 2020, ​President Biden was certified by the Electoral College."

Trump's nominee to trade court apologizes for past social media posts

title: Garlic powder recalled over bacteria risk that can cause diarrhea
description: Heavenly Spices garlic powder was recalled due to possible Bacillus cereus contamination.
fullText:
(NewsNation) — A garlic powder sold across Canada has been recalled due to potential bacterial contamination.

The Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) issued the recall due to concerns that Heavenly Spices garlic powder may have been infected with Bacillus cereus.

The affected 70-gram containers of garlic powder are marked with the identification number RA-82337. They were sold at Dollarama stores throughout Canada, CFIA said.

The recall is classified as Class 2, indicating a moderate risk that consuming the food may cause short-term or nonlife-threatening health problems, according to the CFIA.

Bacillus cereus causes food poisoning, with symptoms including vomiting, nausea, diarrhea and stomach pain that can last about two days. The bacteria can be found in dairy products, fish, rice and potatoes.

If left untreated, complications from the bacteria can spread throughout the body to different organs, though it is rare.

Those who purchased the affected garlic powder can contact Dollarama for a $2.00 e-gift card as a replacement, a spokesperson for the retailer told CTV News.

The garlic powder recall comes as American shoppers avoid produce, specifically lettuce, amid a growing outbreak of cyclosporiasis.

According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 1,645 people have been sickened by cyclosporiasis, along with 141 hospitalizations across 34 states in the U.S. as a result of the outbreak, which causes severe diarrhea along with other symptoms such as cramps, nausea, fatigue and vomiting.

Taylor Farms previously expanded a voluntary recall of iceberg lettuce products sourced from central Mexico after health officials linked the company's products to the outbreak investigation.

The company said it completed its voluntary recall of iceberg lettuce from central Mexico out of an abundance of caution, based on the initial information provided by health officials. Taylor Fresh Foods added that all other Taylor Farms products, including everything sold under the Taylor Farms brand, remain unaffected.

NewsNation's Jordan Perkins contributed to this report.

title: Student raped woman and forced her to complete his assignments
description: Chinese citizen Ziyi Shi, 21, attacked and raped the woman at student accommodation in Edinburgh.
fullText:
A student who raped an undergraduate and forced her to complete his university assignments during a campaign of violent abuse has been jailed.

Chinese citizen Ziyi Shi, 21, attacked the student and subjected her to repeated sexual abuse at student accommodation in Edinburgh.

Shi denied a series of charges during a trial at the High Court in Edinburgh, but was convicted of three crimes of rape, abusive behaviour and breaching an undertaking not to contact the victim.

He was remanded in custody and will be sentenced next month.

Judge Thomas Welsh KC told jurors it was a "distressing" case.

The court heard Shi launched a campaign of abuse towards the victim in September 2022 which continued until January 2024, during which he made derogatory remarks towards her and criticised her appearance.

On one occasion he bit her when she refused to have intercourse with him or let him examine her mobile phone.

During another incident he repeatedly kicked her, slapped her face, gripped her by the neck and poured water on her clothing and camera.

He also made her complete assignments he was given as an electronics and electrical engineering student at the University of Edinburgh.

The court heard that the victim was taking a different course.

Shi also subjected the woman to sexual assaults and rapes between October 2022 and November 2023 when she was asleep or intoxicated with alcohol and incapable of consenting to sex.

On one occasion in March 2023 he attacked her at halls of residence, removed her underwear, grabbed hold of her and raped her.

In another attack on 30 November or 1 December in 2023, he seized the woman by the neck, choked her, repeatedly slapped her face, threatened her and raped her.

The woman told a student adviser that she was scared after being attacked by Shi and had consensual sex with him on other occasions.

Judge Welsh told Shi his crimes were "very serious and grave offences."

The judge called for the preparation of a background report and risk assessment on the first offender ahead of sentencing him next month.

Shi was suspended by the university after he was charged and was previously granted bail after he surrendered his passport.

He was also placed on the sex offenders' register.

title: Speaker Johnson wants to ban members of Congress from dating staffers full stop
description: Speaker Mike Johnson doesn’t think members of Congress should be romantically involved with House staffers — even if the staffers don’t work for them.
fullText:
Speaker Mike Johnson doesn't think members of Congress should be romantically involved with House staffers — even if the staffers don't work for them.

Johnson weighed in during a news conference Tuesday on what has been a hotly debated topic on Capitol Hill: Should members of Congress be allowed to pursue relationships with staffers outside of their own offices? Johnson said it's an easy call for him: They should not.

"I can't believe it would be acceptable in any situation," Johnson said of lawmakers dating any staffers in the House. "I think we should as a bare minimum in Congress have rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America, so that is an easy one for me."

Johnson's comments come as efforts to reduce harassment on Capitol Hill are ramping up and as a bipartisan working group is targeting reforms aimed at making Congress a safer and more professional environment for women.

The issue of whether members should be allowed to date staffers outside their office has been at the center of a debate about how to stamp out sexual harassment on the Hill. Many members argued in the days after the resignations of Democratic Rep. Eric Swalwell and Republican Rep. Tony Gonzales that lawmakers should be barred from any sexual relationships with House staff — not just those who worked for them.

But the effort has come up against some critics who argued that with more young and single lawmakers coming to Washington, consensual relationships outside of a members' office were beyond the bounds of what Congress should police.

Democratic Women's Caucus Chair Teresa Leger Fernandez swiftly applauded Johnson's position, arguing it should be included in a slate of proposals from the bipartisan working group on sexual harassment.

"From the beginning, we've pushed to extend the prohibition against members having sexual relationships to ALL staff," she said. "The power dynamic that leads to abuse exists for all staffers, not just those within the members own office."

She added she looks forward to "finalizing our work" with the Republican Women's Caucus "to reform the culture of sexual misconduct on the Hill."

It is already against House rules for members to pursue or date their own staff, but Johnson's comments may give the bipartisan working group more momentum to pursue the effort.

GOP Rep. Kat Cammack told CNN before Johnson's comments Tuesday that the working group was about "70%" there on effort to roll out a list of proposals.

But she did argue there was still some work to do.

"There's a lot of consensus around the training. There's really good consensus on awareness and streamlining. Now the hard part, which is how do you address consequent accountability and ending the retaliation on Capitol Hill?" she said.

One of the more delicate parts of the negotiation has been how to deal with lawmakers who break the rules, how quickly they should be held accountable, and how to balance the interest in accountability with the fact that members are elected by voters.

"You have to balance the constitutional role that we have in protecting people back home and their right to representation and bad behavior up here on Washington in on Capitol Hill," Cammack said. "That to me is probably one of the most frustrating things, is if constituents back home knew how their member was operating up here on Capitol Hill, the people back home would take care of business in the following election. But unfortunately … we don't wear body cameras up here."

title: Former Clinton official warns 'radical' socialist takeover will 'destroy' the Democratic Party if unchecked
description: Jamie Metzl says the Democratic Socialists of America will destroy the Democratic Party unless Hakeem Jeffries and other Democrats lead a clear stand against the far-left movement.
fullText:
A lifelong Democrat and former Clinton administration official issued a warning to his party, arguing that radical socialist factions will "destroy" the Democratic Party if leadership fails to act.

Speaking on "Fox & Friends" Tuesday, Jamie Metzl called on Rep. Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., and party leaders to stand against the threat posed by the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA).

"The DSA is a radical extremist movement trying to take over the Democratic Party," Metzl said. "They want to seize the means of production. They want to eradicate police and prisons. They have a terrible message of discord and hate.

Dem Rep Henry Cuellar Rejects Dsa Agenda, Says Socialists Are Trying To 'Use' Democratic Party

"So, unless the Democratic Party takes on these dangerous, extremist radicals in a very clear and unequivocal way now, we will destroy our own party. And that's not good for the Democrats, and it's not for America."

Fox News Digital reached out to the DSA for comment but did not immediately receive a response.

Metzl described a struggle for power in the Democratic Party between moderate factions and those aligned more closely with the DSA. He urged Jeffries and other Democrats to "lead the charge against these extremist radicals who are running on the Democratic Party line."

Jeffries' Socialism Dilemma: New York Victories Expose Democratic Party Divide

He said Jeffries needs to take a clear stand, arguing Jeffries has been trying to balance criticism of the DSA with what Metzl described as support for some of the group's positions.

"Hakeem Jeffries is trying to strike a balance between being a little bit critical and being supportive, and I understand the motivation because they're trying to gain a majority in the House. But if the Democrats gain a majority of the House miraculously, but do it by an alliance with these radicals, it's going to be very, very dangerous for the Democrats to really do anything," Metzl said.

"Now is the time for the Democrats to say, 'Here's who we are, and here's what we stand for. And the future of the Democratic Party is not accommodating dangerous extremist radicals who are against American democracy.'"

Mamdani Doubles Down On Democratic Socialism While Insisting Wall Street, Business Leaders Can Work With Him

Metzl's warnings come after several democratic socialists won major elections, including in New York and Colorado. Melat Kiros, a new candidate backed by the DSA, pulled off an upset in Colorado, defeating longtime Democratic Rep. Diana DeGette in Denver's heavily Democratic district.

In late June, a group of far-left candidates won their elections in New York with backing from New York City's democratic socialist mayor, Zohran Mamdani. The winners included newcomer Darializa Avila Chevalier, who beat Rep. Adriano Espaillat, and Brad Lander, who defeated Rep. Dan Goldman.

title: 6,600 noncitizens registered to vote after error in New Jersey motor vehicle system, governor says
description: About 6,600 people who indicated they were noncitizens while obtaining New Jersey driver's licenses and other state IDs were improperly registered to vote in 2023 and 2024, Gov. Mikie Sherrill said Tu...
fullText:
HADDONFIELD, New Jersey (AP) — About 6,600 people who indicated they were noncitizens while obtaining New Jersey driver's licenses and other state IDs were improperly registered to vote in 2023 and 2024, Gov. Mikie Sherrill said Tuesday.

The Democratic governor, who took office this year, blamed a Motor Vehicle Commission system software error but said a preliminary analysis showed that fewer than 400 of those who registered that way went on to cast ballots.

The revelation comes as President Donald Trump and other Republicans continue to assert that voting by noncitizens is rampant in U.S. elections, even though it's rare and, when caught, can be punished as a felony that can lead to deportation.

Sherrill promised immediate action and said she has ordered that all erroneously added voter registrations from that period be removed. She also is replacing the vendor who ran the system and is launching an investigation into how the error happened.

"I am appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time," she said in a statement.

She also attempted to head off any future criticism of the issue from the president.

"As the Trump Administration tries to weaponize elections for political gain, I am ensuring we protect our elections," she said.

The White House on Tuesday used the revelation to promote Trump's SAVE America Act, which would require people to show documentary proof of citizenship before they register to vote and impose voter ID requirements nationwide. The measure has passed the U.S. House but has not gotten a vote in the Senate, where even some Republicans oppose it.

"American voters deserve to have confidence that our elections are safe and secure," White House spokesperson Abigail Jackson said in a statement.

Sherrill also noted that the noncitizens who registered did so as Democrats, Republicans and independents. About 6.9 million people in New Jersey are registered to vote and more than 4 million cast ballots in the 2024 presidential election.

Phil Murphy, also a Democrat, was governor when the error occurred. A representative for the former governor did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Christine Hanlon, chair of the Republican party organization in New Jersey, said voter registration needs to be improved and that a bipartisan investigation should dig into what happened, including why the registrations weren't disclosed previously.

"The system and verification process must be improved in order to safeguard our elections," she said in a statement Tuesday. "The public also deserves to know why this was covered up and who was responsible for keeping this critical information from the public."

In a speech last week, Trump touted an investigation from the Department of Homeland Security, which said it had identified about 278,000 noncitizens registered to vote in federal elections across the country. It said most of them were in California, Nevada, New Jersey and Pennsylvania.

Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin on Tuesday suggested it was that report that prompted Sherrill to take action.

"Appreciate you responding back to our letter, @GovSherrillNJ. This should've been done a LONG time ago," he said in a message on X. "Just ONE illegal vote cancels out the vote of a U.S. citizen."

The Pennsylvania Department of State said in an emailed statement provided by Gov. Josh Shapiro's office Tuesday that "evidence has shown that noncitizen voting is extremely rare across the country, including in Pennsylvania." The department added that it would review any information the Department of Homeland Security provided to "evaluate the validity of these claims."

New Jersey is among 19 states that allow people who are in the U.S. illegally to hold driver's licenses and other state-issued ID cards, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. Every state allows noncitizens who have legal status in the U.S. to hold a license.

The NCSL also says that about half the states have automatic voter registration in which eligible people who obtain licenses or, in some cases, other state services, can opt in to voter registration.

In Oregon, officials in 2024 found that about 1,600 people were registered to vote through the state's DMV even though they didn't show proof of citizenship. Most of them were later confirmed to be citizens. The number of people wrongly registered grew by more than 100 by last year, but the state found that only about 30 ineligible voters had cast ballots.

Colorado officials said in 2022 that they had mistakenly sent postcards to 30,000 noncitizens to encourage them to register. The state said none would be allowed if they tried to do so.

A 2019 state audit of California's then-new DMV registration program found numerous errors, some that might have allowed about 1,500 people to incorrectly register to vote. Of those, some might not have been U.S. citizens.

title: iOS 26.6 Comes With Two New Features, and One Is Key for iOS 27
description: You need iOS 26.6 to prime your iPhone for Siri AI.
fullText:
When you think about a big iPhone update on the horizon, iOS 27 likely comes to mind. And yet, Apple is almost ready to release a totally different update: iOS 26.6. Typically, iOS updates this close to the launch of the next major version are focused on security fixes and squashing bugs, saving any major changes for the next big numbered update.

And yet, while iOS 26.6 is a decidedly small update, it's still a notable one, deliveringtwo new features you should be aware of—including one that actually lays the groundwork for iOS 27.

To be clear, iOS 26.6 isn't actually out yet. Apple seeded the "Release Candidate" (RC) to beta testers this week; this is the version of iOS 26.6 the company intends to ship to the general public. If testers find no issues with the update, we'll be getting this version of iOS 26.6 imminently. Otherwise, Apple may make some adjustments. But regardless, these below features are coming to your iPhone with the next update.

iOS 26.6's first feature won't reveal itself when you install the update on your iPhone. In fact, most of us may think iOS 26.6 has no new features at all. But the update's most important new change is key for compatible iPhones upgrading to iOS 27.

As it turns out, iOS 26.6 "optimizes the Spotlight index" on your iPhone to prepare it for iOS 27. In other words, the update gets your iPhone ready for Siri AI. Apple's AI-powered assistant relies on the new search architecture in iOS 27 to run properly. As such, if you install iOS 27 without updating to iOS 26.6 first, it may take days before Siri AI can perform as expected. By preparing your iPhone with iOS 26.6 now, you'll avoid the growing pains.

Of course, that won't benefit everyone eventually upgrading to iOS 27. While the update runs on the iPhone 12 and newer devices, only the iPhone 15 Pro and newer can try Siri AI. Still, if that's you, iOS 27's new Siri will run much more smoothly coming from iOS 26.6 than from iOS 26.5.2.

iOS 26.6's other new feature is a new alert, letting you know that you've reached iOS' limit of the number of contacts you can block. It's not clear exactly how many contacts that is, but new code in the update confirms the alert is present. The alert reads: "Blocked Contacts Limit Reached: You’ve reached the maximum number of blocked contacts. To block additional callers, remove a blocked contact in Settings."

The thing is, there's no documentation of what that limit actually is. In fact, anecdotal evidence suggests that the limit is user-dependent. MacRumors reports that some users receive the alert after blocking 20,000 contacts, while others only get 8,000. I've seen comments suggesting the limit is lower still, with some claiming to hit it in the 5,000s. Nonetheless, that's a lot of blocked contacts, and will likely only apply to users who receive an overwhelming number of calls and messages from unwanted sources.

Why was Hamilton penalised but not Leclerc? - F1 Q&A
description: BBC Sport F1 correspondent Andrew Benson answers your latest questions before the Hungarian Grand Prix.
fullText:
Mercedes' Kimi Antonelli won the Belgian Grand Prix from pole position on Sunday to extend his lead at the top of the drivers' championship to 45 points.

Antonelli's team-mate George Russell retired from the race following an opening-lap collision with Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton.

Russell has now dropped to third in the standings, with Hamilton replacing him in second place after finishing fourth.

Ferrari's Charles Leclerc was second behind Antonelli and Red Bull's Max Verstappen completed the podium in third.

BBC Sport F1 correspondent Andrew Benson answers your latest questions after the race at Spa-Francorchamps.

Antonelli near-perfect but too much drama for Hamilton - driver ratings

Russell 'numb to disappointment' after bitter blow to title hopes

If Lewis Hamilton received a five-second time penalty for what appeared to me to be a racing incident between himself and George Russell, then how did Charles Leclerc get away with the Oscar Piastri incident where he clearly didn't leave enough room for the McLaren driver? - John

The stewards' justifications for these two different incidents were based on a number of factors, many related to the Formula 1 drivers' guidelines.

In the case of the collision between Hamilton and Russell on the first lap, the decision stated that Russell had got his car far enough alongside Hamilton's Ferrari on the outside as they approached the corner to be making a legitimate overtaking attempt and therefore was "entitled to racing room".

They went on to say that the collision between the two cars was triggered by Hamilton's car sliding and that he hit Russell's car despite "applying corrective steering".

Russell "remained within the available racing room and the collision was caused by [Hamilton] being unable to maintain sufficient separation".

Hamilton was therefore "wholly at fault for the collision".

The normal penalty in these circumstances is 10 seconds. This was reduced to five in Hamilton's case because he "acknowledged the presence of [Russell], attempted to leave appropriate racing room and made a genuine attempt to avoid contact".

In addition, the incident was not caused by "an aggressive or deliberate manoeuvre", and it was on the opening lap, when more leeway is applied.

As for the incident between Leclerc and Piastri, again the racing rules were applied to the letter.

The stewards ruled that Piastri was not far enough alongside the Ferrari to be entitled to the same treatment as Russell.

This was because while he had "some overlap", Piastri's "front axle… was not ahead of the front axle [of Leclerc] and there was no possibility of completing an overtake from that position".

Objectively, it's hard to disagree with their conclusion up to that point.

The next part of the ruling was more subjective.

"[Leclerc] did not deliberately crowd [Piastri] off the edge of the track. [He] followed the racing line before the entry to Turn Five by moving slightly to the left… and side to side contact occurred between the two cars".

There was an interesting episode in the green room before the podium. , externalThis incident played out on the television while Leclerc was sitting watching with Max Verstappen and Kimi Antonelli.

Leclerc's eyes widened and he sucked in a breath as he watched the contact, and he said: "This one was…"

Verstappen interrupted and said: "Was calculated." Leclerc looked at him, they both laughed and then Leclerc sat back looking sheepish and put his cap over his face.

It's clear he recognised the move was on the edge, and a case can be made that he did not comply with the rule that says a driver defending their position by changing line must leave a car's width on the outside if they move back to the racing line.

McLaren team principal Andrea Stella was asked for his view after the race.

He said he had not yet reviewed the stewards' reasoning, but added: "When the cars are staying alongside each other for such a long time, we should make sure that the car that is not on the racing line, because it's defending the inside, does leave enough space, because if there's a contact that is not controlled, then the crash is going to be very big."

I'm sure I read somewhere that this weekend was a record attendance for a Belgian Grand Prix. Given that, and the clear love that drivers and fans share for Spa, it seems ridiculous it won't be on the calendar every year, while far less popular circuits are remaining. Does the FIA care about the views of the fans and drivers or is it all about what races pay the most? - Tom

F1 did say that the attendance at the Belgian Grand Prix this year was an all-time record at 400,000 over the weekend as a whole.

And on a point of fact regarding the question, the calendar is compiled by F1, not the FIA, which merely rubber stamps the schedule at its world council.

In determining which races feature, F1 weighs a number of different factors. Of course, finances are one of them, but money is not everything.

Some races pay smaller fees than others, and one of the reasons for this can be that they are considered to have a greater intrinsic value to the sport.

This would apply to Spa, which has a history and circuit layout that surpasses the vast majority of the rest of the calendar.

However, it is the case that the historic European classic tracks such as Spa and Silverstone - and other events such as Austin - simply cannot afford to pay the same fees as races funded by nation states using them for PR purposes, such as Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, for example.

This is not an easy conundrum to resolve. F1 wants tracks such as Spa on the calendar for all the obvious reasons, but they also have to make financial sense.

It is in trying to find an acceptable compromise to this scenario that the latest situation has arisen, with Spa hosting the Belgian Grand Prix for four out of six years of its current contract.

That means that in 2028 and 2030 Spa will be replaced by another race.

Of course, from the point of view of anyone who appreciates the sport and what it's about, that's not ideal. But it's better than having no Spa at all.

Why are Mercedes struggling to understand George Russell's straight-line deficit? They must have all the data on his battery deployment, ICE output, e-motor power, plus how much drag his aero and car set-up, such as rake angle, generates - Sean

At the Belgian Grand Prix, Mercedes experienced unexpected behaviour from their power unit.

Russell suffered for this, by having a mystery lack of energy over a lap in qualifying. It cost him some but not all of his deficit to team-mate Kimi Antonelli. Mercedes team principal Toto Wolff put the loss from the engine at about 0.2-0.25 seconds.

A pretty much identical thing happened at McLaren to Oscar Piastri in relation to Lando Norris.

Both drivers also experienced a shortfall of energy on the first lap that contributed both to Russell's incident with Hamilton and Antonelli being passed by Max Verstappen on the approach to Eau Rouge.

As Wolff said: "All Mercedes engines had a lack of energy out of Turn One so that wasn't only George. It affected him more than Kimi but also Kimi had that issue to a certain degree."

The one in the race was, Wolff said, "a new issue".

The fundamental issue is that the latest F1 hybrid engines are highly complex, as are the rules by which they have to be operated, and the manufacturers and teams are still learning about them.

One of the impacts of this is that small nuances of driving can have a disproportionately large impact on energy availability. It's one of the reasons why the drivers do not like these engines.

McLaren team principal Andrea Stella, among the best technical communicators in the sport, has talked about this extensively this season.

At the weekend in Spa he said: "To operate these power units, you don't operate them in an open-loop way, whereby I would be able to have an off-line simulation and say, 'Oh, that's how the electrical power will be deployed and it will always be identical to itself'.

"There's a large component of the controller that operates the power unit that happens with live calculations and the power unit forward-thinks, forward-calculates what it has to do. There's a lot that happens as the car goes.

"This is why it's not so easy to understand what kind of calculations have been made as the car was going.

"I'm sure we will understand the reason, but at the same time you need to be able to anticipate and make sure that the operation of the power unit unfolds as expected.

"Because this is not only for the influence it has in the power-limited sections, [and] in the straights, but also because it affects the braking points.

"Because if you have an additional amount of harvest before braking, then you are approaching the braking 10km/h slower and your braking point changes.

"This is quite difficult to master for the drivers, and it's one of the reasons why they talk about the difficulties of exploiting the power unit from a driving point of view.

"It's not only to get the most out of the power unit, but also because the variations of the power unit affect the references as you approach a corner.

"But the variations that are significantly relevant are so small that they may happen from one lap to the other, even if you have learned from the previous laps."

This complexity also explains why McLaren have been frustrated this season by some aspects of their relationship with Mercedes, particularly in terms of how much information they have been given about the operation of the engine.

As Stella said: "It's only now, and we are mid-season, that we are starting to have the required tools in order to be able to anticipate, optimise, and study these sensitivities."

Why are drivers allowed to sacrifice their qualifying session to provide a tow to their team-mate? It is understandable during a race, but it feels against the spirit of qualifying - Sylvie

This is a reference to the way Red Bull ran qualifying in Belgium.

Isack Hadjar gave Max Verstappen a tow in the final session that made a difference of about 0.3secs. It allowed Verstappen to qualify on the front row rather than sixth.

If this was to happen routinely, then there might well be consequences.

But this particular station was entirely understandable, and completely normal.

Hadjar had a 30-place grid penalty for exceeding his allowance of power unit components. So, having made it into Q3, he was only ever going to be starting the race at the back, no matter what lap time he set.

In those circumstances, it made complete sense for Red Bull to ask him to give Verstappen a slipstream to maximise his chances.

title: Derry City given suspended partial stadium closure by Uefa
description: Derry City are to be issued a suspended partial closure of Ryan McBride Brandywell Stadium by Uefa.
fullText:
Derry City have been given a suspended partial closure of Ryan McBride Brandywell Stadium by Uefa.

The punishment follows trouble between rival supporters before and during the Candystripes' Europa League match against CSKA Sofia on 16 July, which caused police to be deployed at the stadium.

The game - which Tiernan Lynch's side lost 2-1 to exit the competition - was halted for 14 minutes as fans, including young children, spilled on to the pitch to avoid confrontations in the stands.

The partial stadium closure is suspended for a probationary period of two years.

Derry City have also been fined 20,000 euro (£17,000) over the incidents, while CSKA Sofia have been banned from bringing away supporters to their next European fixture, with a further one-game sanction suspended for two years.

The Bulgarian side have also been issued with fines totalling 55,000 euro (£47,000) and ordered to reach a settlement with City over damage caused to the stadium.

A statement from Derry City said the club had complained to Uefa over "the aggressive and intimidatory behaviour" of visiting supporters but "fully accepted" their own punishment.

"Derry City FC is committed to taking all necessary steps to ensure that home matches are played in an environment that is safe for players and supporters alike.

"An investigation is underway to identify those supporters of Derry City FC who were involved in these incidents and who ultimately caused the club to incur this fine. Once identified, the club will take appropriate steps to sanction these supporters, including the possibility of a lifetime ban from the McBride Stadium.

"Separately, CSKA Sofia has apologised to Derry City FC for the damage caused to the Ryan McBride Stadium by its supporters. CSKA Sofia has agreed to cover the cost of remediating this damage and Derry City FC is liaising with CSKA Sofia on this issue."

Derry return to European action on Thursday night (19:45 BST) when they travel to face Croatian side HNK Rijeka in the second qualifying round of the Conference League.

title: Haveron 'proud' of Larne efforts in Red Star defeat
description: Larne manager Gary Haveron says he is "proud" of his players' efforts in the 4-0 home defeat by Red Star Belgrade in their Champions League second qualifying round, first leg.
fullText:
Larne manager Gary Haveron said he was "proud" of his players' efforts in their 4-0 home defeat by Red Star Belgrade in the Champions League second qualifying round, first leg.

The Inver Reds trailed at the break after Bruno Duarte converted from the spot, but the Serbian club would add another three in the second period as Duarte grabbed a second, while Vasilije Kostov and Muhammed Cham found the net to all but finish the tie off.

Haveron felt his team can take a lot from the experience but accepts they were up against a "different level" of opposition.

"I'm extremely proud," Haveron told BBC Sport NI.

"For long stages in the first half we frustrated them and were probably a bit disappointed to come in one down given the soft penalty.

"I thought the shape was really good, discipline really good and in the second half we got caught up in the occasion and chased the game a bit too much. When you give a quality team space in behind, they can hurt you and did that in a 10-minute spell when they scored two goals and killed the tie.

"It's a learning opportunity for us and we can take so much as a club, coaching staff and players. The quality is different level and some of the finishes were ridiculous."

Larne will travel to Belgrade for the second leg next Wednesday [19:00 BST] and while Haveron accepts the Champions League is "a step too far", he is hopeful they can push on in the Europa or perhaps Conference League qualifiers.

"Our ceiling wasn't the Champions League," he stressed.

"Winning the first game [against Tre Fiori] gave us an opportunity in the Europa League, gave us an opportunity in the Conference League that gives us a level of football we can be competitive in.

"We made group stages previously [2024/25] but you need to get lucky with the draw and it gives us a chance to be competitive.

"When you get to this stage of the Champions League, it's a step too far, but about the occasion, the experience and performance, so next week will stand to us going into the rest of the season."

title: Joshua v Prenga - all you need to know
description: All you need to know about Anthony Joshua's return to the boxing ring against Kristian Prenga in Saudi Arabia on Saturday.
fullText:
Anthony Joshua returns to the ring against Kristian Prenga on Saturday in a warm-up bout for his planned super-fight with fellow British heavyweight Tyson Fury.

Joshua, 36, last fought in December when he beat Jake Paul with a devastating sixth-round knockout in Miami.

Albania's Prenga, 35, carries knockout power but has never established himself as a world level contender in the heavyweight division.

Will Joshua get the win required to keep alive hopes of the fight with Fury? Or can Prenga pull off a major upset?

Here is all you need to know about the bout in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.

Radio commentary from Jeddah will begin from about 20:30 BST on BBC Radio 5 Live, BBC Sounds, and the BBC Sport website and app, on Saturday, 25 July.

Coverage will be presented from ringside by Jonathan Overend, alongside two-weight world champion Natasha Jonas and boxing expert Steve Bunce.

Live text commentary will begin at 19:30 on the BBC Sport website and app.

Briton Hamzah Sheeraz makes the first defence of his WBO super-middleweight title against Germany's Simon Zachenhuber in the co-main event.

Fellow Briton Josh Kelly also defends his IBF light-middleweight belt against Ireland's Caoimhin Agyarko.

We will bring you round-by-round updates for both world title fights and the headline act of Joshua v Prenga.

You can also follow the action through fight week on BBC Sport via the 5 Live Boxing podcast with daily episodes.

IBF light-middleweight champion Kelly and challenger Agyarko are scheduled to make their ringwalks at around 20:40 BST.

The co-main event, which sees Sheeraz make the maiden defence of his WBO super-middleweight title against Zachenhuber, is slated for 21:40.

Joshua and Prenga are not expected to make their walk to the ring before 23:00.

All timings are estimated and are subject to change.

Anthony Joshua v Kristian Prenga - heavyweight

Hamzah Sheeraz (c) v Simon Zachenhuber - WBO super-middleweight title

Josh Kelly (c) v Caoimhin Agyarko - IBF light-middleweight world title

Reito Tsutsumi v Alvino Herrera - super-featherweight

Jacob Bank v Pawel August - super-middleweight

Nishant Dev v Cesar Diaz - light-middleweight

Joshua is just shy of three months older than Prenga, but their careers have followed very different paths.

After winning gold at the 2012 Olympics, Joshua turned professional the following year and went on to become a two-time heavyweight world champion.

Joshua won each of his first 16 fights inside the distance in a run that culminated in him beating Charles Martin to claim the IBF title.

His crowning moment came in 2017 when he stopped Wladimir Klitschko in the 11th round at Wembley to unify titles - adding the WBA belt to his collection.

In 2018, Joshua outpointed Joseph Parker - winning by unanimous decision - to claim the WBO title.

Joshua tasted defeat for the first time against American Andy Ruiz Jr in 2019 but earned revenge to reclaim the titles in a rematch.

He was beaten in successive fights by Oleksandr Usyk in 2021 and 2022, before embarking on a four-fight winning streak to earn the chance to become a three-time world champion.

However, fellow Briton Daniel Dubois, who held the IBF belt, put in a career-best performance to beat Joshua by sixth-round knockout in September 2024 at Wembley Stadium.

Joshua returned to the ring in December 2025 and stopped YouTuber-turned-boxer Paul to take his overall record to 29 wins and four defeats, with 26 of his victories coming by knockout.

Prenga made his professional debut three years after Joshua and won his first four fights by knockout.

The Albanian fell to his first and only loss in his fifth bout and steps into the ring on Saturday on a 16-fight winning streak, although he does not have any recognisable names on his record.

Prenga does carry a knockout threat after picking up all 20 of his victories in that fashion.

There are no belts on the line, but there is a big prize waiting on the other side of the contest for Joshua if he wins.

After a decade-long saga, Joshua and British rival Fury have signed a deal to finally get in the ring.

Fury came out of retirement to beat Arslanbek Makhmudov in April and Joshua was expected to be his next opponent.

But Joshua has not fought since he was a passenger in a fatal car crash in late December and wanted to take a warm-up fight with Prenga first.

Fury has also agreed to a warm-up bout and fights 46-year-old Mariusz Wach in Thailand on Friday.

If both Britons come through victorious then they are expected to share a ring later this year.

A date or venue has not been announced, though Matchroom promoter Eddie Hearn has previously suggested it could land in November on streaming giant Netflix.

Saudi powerbroker Turki Alalshikh, who is co-ordinating the all-British bout, said it could take place at Wembley Stadium if local authorities are happy to stage the event in the early hours of the morning to accommodate a global audience.

Sources close to London mayor Sir Sadiq Khan have told BBC Sport such a proposal is "doable" while bosses of Cardiff's Principality Stadium also said they would be ready to host.

Thursday – news conference from 18:00

title: Britain's Clarke wins kayak gold at World Championship
description: Britain's Joe Clarke marks the opening day of the ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships with a gold medal in the men's kayak cross in Oklahoma City.
fullText:
Britain's Joe Clarke MBE marked the opening day of the ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships with a gold medal in the men's kayak cross in Oklahoma City.

The 33-year-old surged to victory in the individual time trial in 46.54 seconds at the Olympics Canoe Slalom venue set to be used at the 2028 Los Angeles Games.

"It feels amazing. I've not been running the best the past couple of days and I tweaked my neck a little bit," Clarke said.

"Yesterday's kayak cross was probably one of the worst I've ever had, so to turn it around within 16 hours is fantastic and a great start to the competition."

Clarke won silver in the same event at Penrith last year, and has now won seven world titles across all events.

Meanwhile, Lois Leaver claimed her first-ever World Championship medal at senior level as she won bronze in the women's kayak cross individual.

The 24-year-old finished just 0.12 seconds off gold in 51.17 seconds, her best career result to date.

title: Chelsea interested in former Man City defender Stones
description: Chelsea are interested in signing England defender John Stones on a free transfer following his departure from Manchester City.
fullText:
Chelsea are interested in signing England defender John Stones on a free transfer following his departure from Manchester City.

The west London side have joined Inter Milan in considering a move for the 32-year-old, who became a free agent when his City contract expired at the end of June.

Chelsea are also in talks to sign Crystal Palace defender Maxence Lacroix while assessing other options in the market, including Como's Jacobo Ramon.

They are also looking to sell central defenders to make space in their squad for potential new arrivals.

Fellow England international Trevoh Chalobah is in talks to join Como, while Axel Disasi and Benoit Badiashile are among those available for sale.

Stones made 295 appearances for City after joining from Everton for £47.5m in 2016.

He helped the club win six Premier League titles, the Champions League, two FA Cups, five League Cups, three Community Shields, the Club World Cup and the Uefa Super Cup.

The former Barnsley defender also has 93 international caps and was part of the Three Lions side who finished third at the World Cup.

Chelsea announced the £117m signing of England midfielder Morgan Rogers from Aston Villa on Tuesday evening.

The Blues are also in talks with Villa about Argentina winger Alejandro Garnacho moving on loan in the opposite direction.

title: Chelsea, Arsenal and Spurs all want Rashford - Wednesday's gossip
description: A trio of Premier League heavyweights all want Marcus Rashford, Arsenal are interested in Aston Villa's Ezri Konsa, and Nottingham Forest look set to sign a new defender.
fullText:
A trio of Premier League heavyweights all want Marcus Rashford, Arsenal are interested in Aston Villa's Ezri Konsa, and Nottingham Forest look set to sign a new defender.

Tottenham, Chelsea and Arsenal are all plotting a move for Manchester United forward Marcus Rashford, 28, but the Old Trafford hierarchy do not want to sell him to another English club. (The I - subscription required), external

Aston Villa's Argentina goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez, 33, is a target for Juventus while Arsenal are interested in Villa's 28-year-old England defender Ezri Konsa. (Telegraph - subscription required), external

Nottingham Forest are in advanced talks to sign Sporting CP and Ivory Coast defender Ousmane Diomande, 22. (Fabrizio Romano), external

West Ham's French centre-back Jean-Clair Todibo, 26, is wanted by Juventus. (Gazzetta dello Sport - in Italian), external

Manchester United remain keen to sign a central midfielder but have cooled their interest in Brighton's Cameroon international Carlos Baleba and Bournemouth's Guernseyman Alex Scott, both 22, because of their high price tags. (Teamtalk), external

Bournemouth are determined to keep hold of Scott, who is interesting Arsenal, even though the England Under-21 international has rejected a new contract with the Cherries. (Telegraph - subscription required), external

Ipswich have agreed a deal in principle to sign Celtic's Japanese forward Daizen Maeda, 28, for £8m plus £2m in add-ons. (Sky Sports), external

Manchester United's 18-year-old Australian full-back James Overy is being monitored by Rangers and several clubs in Australia. (Mail Plus - subscription required), external

title: Iraola wants Mac Allister & Alisson to stay with Liverpool
description: Liverpool boss Andoni Iraola wants midfielder Alexis Mac Allister and goalkeeper Alisson to remain at the club.
fullText:
Liverpool boss Andoni Iraola wants midfielder Alexis Mac Allister and goalkeeper Alisson to remain at the club.

Argentina international Mac Allister has two years left on the five-year contract he signed at Liverpool when he joined the Reds in 2023.

The 27-year-old has yet to begin talks over a new deal, which has prompted speculation he could leave Anfield this summer.

However, Iraola told a news conference in Chicago at the beginning of Liverpool's pre-season tour of the United States that he wants to hang on to the player.

"I think Alexis has been one of the best players at the club for the last few years, and he has had a very good World Cup, so it is normal that other clubs want our best players," Iraola said.

"It always happens in other markets. But I want to keep my good players. I'm looking forward more to signing new players rather than losing the ones we have here."

Alisson has a year left on his contract at Anfield and has been linked with a move to Juventus, but Iraola is keen to bank on the 33-year-old Brazilian's experience in the forthcoming season.

"I'm very happy with the situation we have right now in the goalkeeping area," explained the Reds boss.

"Alisson is someone also in a changing room that has lost some references in terms of experience, I think he's someone that definitely will help us in this process of adaptation of the new signings, of the new players, the young players.

"We need the references like Virgil [van Dijk], like Alisson, like Joe Gomez, players that have been here for a long time."

Iraola acknowledged he has one eye on bringing in defensive reinforcements during the summer transfer window, particularly at right-back given Conor Bradley is "months" away from playing.

The Northern Ireland international sustained a knee injury in January and will not be available for the start of the season.

"At the end, if you have a fully fit, healthy Conor Bradley, we are very happy, ready to go and we would not be asking ourselves these questions," explained the Spaniard.

"I think it's a situation that is tricky, because we trust Conor in the long term, we know we want him to be our right-back but right now he is still, I would say, a little bit far from returning.

"So, with the centre-back, with the right-back situation, in terms of defensive depth, we are, I think, very thin right now so I think it's a solution that we are trying to find, yes."

title: What can Hearts pick from debris of ongoing summer of hurt?
description: There's crashing back to Earth with a bang, and there's whatever Hearts fans have been going through since 16 May.
fullText:
There's crashing back to Earth with a bang, and there's whatever Hearts fans have been going through since 16 May.

As if losing out on their first league title in 60 years with moments left of the season wasn't bad enough, they have been unceremoniously stripped for parts by Rangers.

Captain, head coach, and talisman all gone, in that order.

Soon though, it would be a new dawn, a new day, and a new man to throw their weight behind as they continue to try and upset the apple cart of Scottish football.

Wouter Vrancken arrived as head coach, seemingly a coup from the continent.

But in his first game in charge on Tuesday, Hearts were battered 4-0 by Sturm Graz and any hopes of Champions League progression have died off quicker than you can say Christian Borchgrevink.

But is there cause for optimism in the underlying stats of the Belgian coach's first game in charge? After all, such data is Jamestown Analytics stock-in-trade...

Hearts collapse to 4-0 Champions League qualification defeat at Sturm Graz

Visit our Hearts page for all the latest news, analysis and fan views

It's been a summer of loss, grief and rebirth at Tynecastle.

It started barely a week after the 3-1 loss to Celtic that will - if those who can remember 1986 can tell you - haunt for years to come.

Hearts could barely have lost three more influential figures from the previous campaign, and each departure provided a blow that stung more than the last.

First it was captain and top scorer Lawrence Shankland. "At least we'll get a fee," some optimists might have thought. Nah, not even that.

A clause in his three-year contract, only signed the summer prior, allowed him to walk away for nothing.

Then, as Scotland were about to kick off their first World Cup campaign in almost three decades, there was the news that head coach Derek McInnes was also on his way to Ibrox.

OK, well, two boyhood Rangers fans. Perhaps it was to be expected.

But bad things come in threes, don't they?

Social media whispers suggested talisman and fan favourite Cammy Devlin was on the cusp of signing a new contract in Gorgie.

A searing statement on X from the club's official account soon ruled out a return, but 24 hours later, he was holding up a royal blue shirt and beaming in front of the camera.

If the days of Hearts lagging behind the Old Firm in the league are gone, the days of them being pillaged by the big two in Glasgow are not.

Before Devlin had even departed, there was a new head coach in charge. The process to appoint Vrancken was quick and initial signs were promising.

Here was a man who had been named Belgian coach of the year, had taken Racing Genk to within minutes of the title, and had been linked with Anderlecht and Burnley.

As well as a glowing CV, his previous style of play in Belgium was highlighted. It was not quite labelled all-out attack, but it was certainly going to be the style of football fans want to see.

Explaining his tactics in an early supporter forum, the man himself said he wanted "chaos within a structure".

In his first match in charge it was mostly chaos, not so much structure.

However, the 4-0 scoreline does not feel truly reflective of the game itself. The attacking stats showed Hearts did not lag behind their Austrian hosts by any means.

They created two chances in the first half that should have been buried, but Pierre Landry Kabore shot straight at the keeper and Alexandros Kyziridis nudged his effort wide when one-on-one.

"Early impressions are that Hearts have got a really good head coach," said Hearts Standard editor Joel Sked on a recent Scottish Football Podcast. "At the same time, just with everything that's happened, there will have to be some patience."

Patience is in short supply in football these days. For every Ange Postecolgou, there's a Wilfried Nancy. For every Jens Berthel Askou, a Russell Martin.

It is too soon to tell in which camp Vrancken will fall, but for the most part, Hearts fans remain cautiously optimistic.

They've been through it all in the past two months, nothing can surprise them. Not even this.

title: All-round Curran leads MI London to dominant win
description: Sam Curran takes two wickets and hits an unbeaten half-century as MI London beat Sunrisers Leeds in the men's Hundred opener.
fullText:
The Hundred men's competition, The Oval

Sunrisers Leeds 143 (99 balls): Marsh 41 (27); Gleeson 4-25, Curran 2-27

MI London 144-3 (84 balls): Curran 60* (39), Pooran 58* (22); Carse 2-18

Sam Curran's all-round brilliance led MI London to a convincing seven-wicket win over Sunrisers Leeds in the opening match of the men's Hundred.

Chasing 144, Curran finished unbeaten on 60 as MI London eased to their target with 16 balls remaining.

After Brydon Carse's fine spell of 2-18 had given Sunrisers hope, Curran added 93 from just 40 balls for the fourth wicket with Nicholas Pooran.

The destructive Pooran struck a magnificent 19-ball fifty which took the game from Sunrisers, as he clubbed five fours and five sixes in his 58 not out.

Curran had earlier taken 2-27 and seamer Richard Gleeson also impressed with 4-25 as Sunrisers were bowled out for 143.

Mitchell Marsh top-scored with 41 at the top of the order but Sunrisers struggled for momentum after his departure, though Tom Alsop's cameo of 31 from 18 balls gave them a late boost.

England white-ball captain Harry Brook fell for eight and Sunrisers skipper Zak Crawley only managed 13.

MI London, who were previously known as Oval Invincibles prior to the private investment in the Hundred, have won the past three men's tournaments.

title: Kerr on why Commonwealth mile glory matters to him
description: Josh Kerr didn't dream about being the fastest person ever to run a mile when he was a kid. He dreamed of pulling on a Scotland vest in the Commonwealth Games.
fullText:
Josh Kerr didn't dream about being the fastest person ever to run a mile when he was a kid. He dreamed of pulling on a Scotland vest and representing the country - his country - in the Commonwealth Games.

That wee boy is now a big man. Arguably the biggest in middle-distance running following his staggering evisceration of the men's mile world record on Saturday.

And now the 28-year-old has a chance to not only fulfil his childhood dream, but to do it by winning gold in Glasgow in front of his own people and at a track that holds memories stretching back a couple of decades.

Speak to Kerr about Scotstoun Stadium and he'll tell you about being windswept, soaked and sunburnt all in the same race. About scrapping with kids from Kilbarchan. About learning how to race not just your rivals but the conditions, too.

"I think some of my biggest performances and learning curves were there," he muses, speaking a week or two out from his record-breaking London outing.

"I've battled there for years and years and it will be nice to be the one that knows the wind and the rain and all that much better than anyone else."

Kerr sees potential to better mile record

Britain's Kerr breaks 27-year mile world record in London

Kerr's compatriots Jake Wightman and Neil Gourley might quibble with that claim, mind, having endured their own share of west of Scotland weather.

All three will be eyeing podium places in the reinstated mile event on 1 August, but Kerr is the only one of the three pals in prime physical condition and is, clearly, the big ticket in the field for the iconic event.

Representing Scotland - and being Scottish - matters to him. He travels home from his New Mexico base four of five times a year. Drops in on his old club on many of those trips. Is on the phone to his family in Edinburgh every day.

And he comes home this summer eager to be right in amongst it with the rest of Team Scotland and with a wrong to right.

Kerr is big on building narratives - the Project 222 branding of his world record attempt was unlikely anything athletics has seen before - and has already sketched out his story for the Commonwealths.

He was a spectator at Glasgow 2014, watching Wightman. Being overlooked four years later for Gold Coast was one of the biggest disappointments of his career, he says. Then an untimely blow-up in Birmingham in 2022, finishing last in the 1500m.

Glasgow will be his redemption.

"This is something we were dreaming about as kids," Kerr says. "I was in a Scottish development squad talking about the Commonwealth Games all the time and that is the kind of stuff that you don't forget.

"Being part of Team Scotland is just everything I wanted to do when I got into the sport. You should never drift too far away from that and I never have. Growing up, I wanted to be standing on that start line with my Scotland vest on.

"I mean, I love it. I put it up with... you've got the Olympics, the worlds, and the Commonwealth Games. That's the way I've always viewed it and if ever I get a call up for the Commonwealths I'd be the first one there.

"Time goes by, and I've lived out in the States for a long time, but these are my roots and this is my blood and I just want to do myself proud and do Scotland proud."
